findings.title: Prefer a named read-range result instead of the new `[2][]uint64` tuple
findings.why: The renamed helper no longer communicates what each slot means. Callers now have to know that `[0]` is the per-file start offsets and `[1]` is the per-file property end offsets, but the doc comment still says the function returns only an offsets list per file for each key.
findings.risk_if_unchanged: Future callers can easily swap the tuple indexes or read `readRangesPerKey` as a complete range object, which makes this helper harder to reuse safely outside the current call sites.
findings.evidence: `getReadRangeFromProps` now returns `[][2][]uint64`, while the changed call sites immediately consume magic indexes (`readRangesPerKey[start][0]`, `readRangesPerKey[end-1][1]`, `readRanges[0][0]`, `readRanges[1][1]`). The updated comment at `util.go` still describes only the old single-offset behavior, so the tuple layout is not documented where the API is declared.
findings.change_request: Prefer a named result type or struct with fields like `startOffsets` and `endOffsets`, and update the helper comment to describe the two returned positions explicitly instead of relying on `[0]` and `[1]`.

findings.title: Precomputing read ranges for every job key can exhaust memory before the first batch starts
findings.why: This now materializes a full `jobKeys x statsFiles x 2` offset table up front, and that allocation sits outside the engine's existing `memLimit`/buffer-pool controls.
findings.risk_if_unchanged: Large imports can hit heavy GC pressure or OOM before `LoadIngestData` emits any batch. The blast radius grows with both split-key count and file count, so the regression shows up exactly on the large-cloud-import workloads this path is meant to serve.
findings.evidence: `LoadIngestData` now calls `getReadRangeFromProps(ctx, e.jobKeys, e.statsFiles, e.storage)` once before entering the batching loop. `getReadRangeFromProps` then allocates two `[]uint64` arrays per job key, each sized to `len(paths)`. That is 16 bytes per `(jobKey, statsFile)` pair before slice overhead. The engine comments in the same file explicitly call out workloads with up to 500K SST files; even 64 job keys at that scale is already about 512 MiB of offset storage, and real region/split-key counts can be much higher.
findings.change_request: Can we keep this bounded by the active batch instead of materializing the whole matrix? A per-batch scan, a sliding window, or a per-file cursor/cache would preserve the optimization without introducing an unbounded allocation that bypasses `memLimit`. Please add a scale-focused test or validation case for large `jobKeys x statsFiles` inputs.

findings.title: readAllData now leaks stats-prop preparation into every caller
findings.why: This refactor turns a local reader concern into a package-wide calling contract: callers must prefetch prop ranges and keep two positionally related slices (`startOffsets` and `endOffsets`) aligned with the file lists.
findings.risk_if_unchanged: Future callers can mix key boundaries with the wrong offset slice, or miss the prep step entirely, which makes the read path harder to audit and raises the chance of partial-read or over-read regressions when this logic changes again.
findings.evidence: The same `getReadRangeFromProps(...); readAllData(..., readRanges[0][0], readRanges[1][1], ...)` sequence is now repeated in both production and test code, and `readAllData` depends on four correlated slice arguments (`dataFiles`, `statsFiles`, `startOffsets`, `endOffsets`) staying in sync.
findings.change_request: Seems no need to push prop lookup into every caller; can we keep one canonical helper or pass a named read-range object so `readAllData` still owns this storage-specific preparation?

findings.title: `readAllData`'s new `endOffsets` parameter name overstates what the function actually does
findings.why: The new signature reads as if both offset arrays bound the actual file reads, but only `startOffsets` is passed to `readOneFile`. `endOffsets` is used only to estimate size and pick concurrency.
findings.risk_if_unchanged: The next maintainer can infer that `readAllData` now enforces both offset bounds and reuse the API under that assumption, even though the stop condition is still `endKey` inside `readOneFile`.
findings.evidence: After the signature change, `readAllData` computes `size := endOffsets[i] - startOffsets[i]` and logs `endOffset`, but the downstream call at `reader.go:111-126` still forwards only `startOffsets[fileIdx]` into `readOneFile`. No name or comment explains that `endOffsets` is only a sizing or concurrency hint.
findings.change_request: Please rename this parameter to something that matches its role, such as `rangeEstimateEndOffsets`, or add a short why-comment stating that the values are only used for concurrency estimation and logging, not as hard read bounds.

findings.title: Package-global concurrency knob is introduced only to support one test seam
findings.why: The new mutable `getReadRangeFromPropsConcurrency` variable exists so `util_test.go` can override the helper's limit, which adds shared package state to production code for a single test case.
findings.risk_if_unchanged: The helper's behavior now depends on ambient package state instead of explicit inputs, which is harder to reason about and can create order-dependent tests if more cases ever need to tweak the same limit.
findings.evidence: Production code reads `getReadRangeFromPropsConcurrency` in `getReadRangeFromProps`, while `TestGetReadRangeFromPropsLimitsParallelRead` mutates and restores that package variable around the call. There is no production path in this diff that needs the limit to vary dynamically.
findings.change_request: Why not use a private helper that accepts the limit, or inject the limit through a test-only seam? That keeps the production path deterministic without another package-global knob.

findings.title: Fixed sleep makes the new concurrency-limit test timing-dependent
findings.why: The determinism checklist explicitly calls out sleep-based synchronization. This test already has an explicit signal for the condition it cares about, so the extra 100ms delay makes the assertion depend on wall-clock time instead of only on the synchronization it introduced.
findings.risk_if_unchanged: The suite pays unnecessary wall-clock cost in CI, and this test becomes an example of timing-based coordination in a path that is otherwise written with explicit synchronization.
findings.evidence: `blockingOpenMemStorage.Open` updates `s.max` before it sends on `startedCh`, and the test already waits for two `startedCh` events before asserting `store.max.Load() == 2`. The new `time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)` therefore does not validate a new condition; it only inserts timing into the test.
findings.change_request: Seems no need to sleep here; can we remove the fixed delay and assert immediately after the two `startedCh` events, or use `require.Eventually` if you want to keep the assertion tied to an explicit condition instead of wall-clock waiting?
